titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 2 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) to his wife, Lillian (1880-1968). Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War."

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/204

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
2 April My p r a very bright day outside, but a good deal
of worry in . R I believe is being trans-
ferred to London at an early date & I believe
the London man is coming here. Not
very hopeful, & he probably knows less of
the war than I do now. Really it is
a thin farce, but no doubt it is part of
the day's work. Yet when I see those
who are getting of on — men I know — it
makes one a lttle disheartened. However
Ulster — with a — — thrown in ! ! ! — is worth it
jolly well. Boylan Smith has just bet £10
to £1 with me that Carson won't be premier in
the year. I should like to get his subscription
to your fund ! When the new man comes & is settled in I shall
really try to get away on leave, but it is
extremely unlikely I shall get it, tho'
there does not seem the same mad haste
as about things that there was lately. This is what I
wrote about, & it is a great relief
to find that sounder counsels prevail now
that we have a real good C in C. I was a little disappointed in Carson's
speech. It appears to me that Bonar
Law, that man of supremely honest
Convictions has persuaded him to hold his
hand , & tho' I know he is absolutely loyal
I do not think his judgement is as sound as
Carson's. The loathing for Squiffy out here is
intense; to men in action an inactive man
is like water to fire, it makes it hiss &
often puts it out if brought into touch with it . I think I could find a good simile
to the Irish situation in the anatomy
of a man:— the entrails the Sinn feiners
who suck in all they can & squeeze; the heart the sentimental Home rulers,
& their joint conspiracy to leave the head
with its balance (bank & judicial). I'm afraid the Division that took
over from us were too much rushed, but
luckily the Huns did not take advantage of
it, though thoug it is bad for young troops
sometimes. The man who is coming is a youngster
a protégé of the ACArmy Commander. I
could get it altered if I wished by going
to R who is a time-server & telling him
of my intimacy with Sir E, but I'm
hanged if I put forward a finger — much
less a dirty finger — in the matter. No letter today & no papers, but
I don't mind so much on Sundays. Yes the b w does want his p r
extra bad ever your b w W.BSpender
